content: The user has provided revision instructions for a chart that you previously generated. Your first task is to decide—based ONLY on the user's revision instructions—whether: (1) the chart/table itself must be changed, OR (2) only your written commentary/title must be updated. Do NOT assume the chart/table is already acceptable. Do NOT assume the user wants commentary-only changes unless they explicitly say so. If the instruction mentions ANY change to parameters, axes, filters, colours, markers, trends, visibility, or data, then you MUST treat it as requiring a chart/table modification. If you determine that the chart/table must be changed, provide explicit and concrete instructions describing exactly how the chart or table must be changed. The user's revision instructions are: The default view is too zoomed in on one area. The default zoom should show the full area of LSOA where there are pesticide detections The following information is provided for reference only and does not imply that the chart is complete or correct. This is the chart the user has provided revision instructions for: analysis_index: 24 item_index: 2 ####Chart: LSOA heat map of pesticide detection counts The LSOA heat map shows that quantified pesticide detections are geographically sparse and highly clustered. Most LSOAs with detections have only 1–2 events over 2022. A small number of LSOAs stand out: Colchester 019A (E01021643) with 7 detections, and several nearby Colchester LSOAs with 2–4 detections, indicating a localised area of repeated low‑level pesticide presence at consumers’ taps. South Cambridgeshire 003E (E01035555) and Central Bedfordshire 018D (E01017616) also show 6 detections each, while a Milton Keynes cluster around codes 032C–032F has 2–4 detections. Doncaster and Bassetlaw LSOAs in the north of the region show isolated detections (1–6 counts). There are no large contiguous high‑count zones, suggesting that pesticide occurrence is driven by specific catchments or sources rather than system‑wide contamination. Chart image: . Code used to create component: ```python # filename: lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.py import pandas as pd import numpy as np import plotly.express as px # Main water quality data filename water_quality_file = "Anglian_Water_Domestic_Water_Quality.csv" # Load main data df = pd.read_csv(water_quality_file, encoding="utf-8") # Convert Sample_Date to datetime df['Sample_Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Sample_Date'], format="%d/%m/%Y %H:%M", errors='coerce') # Filter pesticide determinands: mask_pesticides = df['Determinand'].str.startswith("Pesticides ", na=False) | (df['Determinand'] == "Pesticides (Total by Calculation)") df_pesticides = df.loc[mask_pesticides].copy() # Define detections: Operator not '<' df_pesticides['Is_Detect'] = ~df_pesticides['Operator'].eq('<') # Calculate detection counts per determinand detection_counts = df_pesticides.groupby('Determinand')['Is_Detect'].sum() valid_determinands = detection_counts[detection_counts > 0].index # Filter only to valid detected pesticide determinands and only detects df_valid_detections = df_pesticides[(df_pesticides['Determinand'].isin(valid_determinands)) & (df_pesticides['Is_Detect'])] # Group by LSOA to count detections lsoa_counts = df_valid_detections.groupby('LSOA').size().reset_index(name='detection_count') # Load ONS LSOA centroid CSV ons_lsoa_url = "https://open-geography-portalx-ons.hub.arcgis.com/api/download/v1/items/b8263c2364e9452483a0e5783c6fdb53/csv?layers=0" df_lsoa = pd.read_csv(ons_lsoa_url, encoding='utf-8') # Identify latitude and longitude column names (try common variants) lat_cols = [col for col in df_lsoa.columns if col.lower() in {'lat', 'latitude', 'y'}] lon_cols = [col for col in df_lsoa.columns if col.lower() in {'lon', 'long', 'longitude', 'x'}] if not lat_cols or not lon_cols: raise ValueError("Latitude and/or Longitude columns not found in LSOA centroids data.") lat_col = lat_cols[0] lon_col = lon_cols[0] # Select and rename to common names df_lsoa_selected = df_lsoa[['LSOA21CD', 'LSOA21NM', lat_col, lon_col]].rename( columns={lat_col: 'lat', lon_col: 'lon'} ) # Inner join detection counts with LSOA centroids on code df_map = pd.merge(lsoa_counts, df_lsoa_selected, how='inner', left_on='LSOA', right_on='LSOA21CD') # Sort by detection_count ascending so higher counts are plotted last (on top) df_map = df_map.sort_values('detection_count') # Marker size scale: detection_count mapped from [min, max] -> [5, 25] min_size = 5 max_size = 25 min_count = df_map['detection_count'].min() max_count = df_map['detection_count'].max() # Avoid division by zero if all counts are same if max_count == min_count: df_map['marker_size'] = (min_size + max_size) / 2 else: df_map['marker_size'] = min_size + (df_map['detection_count'] - min_count) / (max_count - min_count) * (max_size - min_size) # Use Plotly Mapbox scatter plot for heat map style # Use open source map style: "open-street-map" fig = px.scatter_mapbox( df_map, lat='lat', lon='lon', size='marker_size', size_max=max_size, color='detection_count', color_continuous_scale='YlOrRd', hover_name='LSOA21NM', hover_data={'LSOA21CD': True, 'detection_count': True, 'lat': False, 'lon': False, 'marker_size': False}, zoom=9, height=600, width=1000, labels={'detection_count': 'Detection Count'}, mapbox_style='open-street-map' ) # Update layout to remove title and add black lines (axes lines are not shown on mapbox plots) fig.update_layout( font=dict(size=16), margin={"r":0,"t":0,"l":0,"b":0}, coloraxis_colorbar=dict(title="Detections"), ) # Save outputs csv_file = "lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap_data.csv" html_file = "lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.html" png_file = "lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.png" df_map[['LSOA21CD', 'LSOA21NM', 'detection_count', 'lat', 'lon']].to_csv(csv_file, index=False) fig.write_html(html_file, include_plotlyjs='cdn', full_html=True) fig.write_image(png_file, width=1000, height=600) print(csv_file) print(html_file) print(png_file) print("lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.py") ``` This chart has been produced from source data as follows: Source data file name: Anglian_Water_Domestic_Water_Quality.csv Data description: Anglian Water domestic drinking water quality dataset for 2022, containing laboratory/sample results at consumer tap locations, with determinand codes, units, operators/qualifiers, numeric results, and Lower Layer Super Output Area (LSOA) identifiers. Sheet name: CSV sheet Headings: Sample_Id, Sample_Date, DWI_Code, Determinand, Units, Operator, Result, LSOA, Data_Provider, ObjectId Time period: 03/01/2022 to 30/12/2022 Time step: Irregular sampling dates (up to daily) Time format: %d/%m/%Y Here is additional information on the data file: # Data description for Anglian_Water_Domestic_Water_Quality.csv ## CSV Encoding: The expected CSV charts and tables that have been created from this dataset. You will not be revising these. ####Table: Pesticide summary statistics and UK PCVs The summary table shows that most pesticide determinands are present only as non-detects in 2022; for many (e.g. aldrin, dieldrin, heptachlor, heptachlor epoxide, atrazine, bentazone) there were zero quantified detections despite multiple samples. Total pesticides (by calculation) were measured 45 times with all quantified values ≤0.037 µg/L, well below the 0.50 µg/L total pesticides PCV. Among specific compounds with detections, metaldehyde (17 detects, max 0.037 µg/L, median 0.024 µg/L) and propyzamide (4 detects, max 0.024 µg/L, median 0.015 µg/L) are the main contributors. All quantified pesticide concentrations remain below their respective individual PCVs (0.1 µg/L for general pesticides, 0.03 µg/L for the listed organochlorines). From a compliance perspective, the 2022 dataset indicates a low pesticide burden with no exceedances of UK regulatory limits. Pirimicarb,P064,μg/l ,2,0,,,,,0.1, Pesticides Prometryne,P070,μg/l ,2,0,,,,,0.1, Pesticides Propazine,P066,μg/l ,2,0,,,,,0.1, Pesticides Propyzamide (Total),P071,μg/l ,5,4,0.024,0.015,0.024,0.015,0.1, Pesticides Quinmerac,P244,μg/l ,2,0,,,,,0.1, Pesticides Simazine (Total),P073,μg/l ,5,0,,,,,0.1, Pesticides Terbutryn,P077,μg/l ,2,0,,,,,0.1, Pesticides Triclopyr (Total),P131,μg/l ,2,0,,,,,0.1, Pesticides Trietazine,P132,μg/l ,24,0,,,,,0.1, Code used to create component: ```python # filename: pesticides_summary_stats.py import pandas as pd import numpy as np file_name = "Anglian_Water_Domestic_Water_Quality.csv" # Load data df = pd.read_csv(file_name, encoding="utf-8") # Filter pesticide determinands: # Determinand starts with "Pesticides " OR equals "Pesticides (Total by Calculation)" mask_pesticides = df['Determinand'].str.startswith("Pesticides ", na=False) | (df['Determinand'] == "Pesticides (Total by Calculation)") df_pesticides = df.loc[mask_pesticides].copy() # Treat Operator: # Operator == '<' means non-detect; exclude from median and max calculations df_pesticides['Is_Detect'] = ~df_pesticides['Operator'].eq('<') # Calculate counts agg = df_pesticides.groupby(['Determinand', 'DWI_Code', 'Units']).agg( n_results=('Result', 'size'), n_detects=('Is_Detect', 'sum'), max_detect=('Result', lambda x: x[df_pesticides.loc[x.index, 'Is_Detect']].max() if x[df_pesticides.loc[x.index, 'Is_Detect']].size > 0 else np.nan), median_detect=('Result', lambda x: x[df_pesticides.loc[x.index, 'Is_Detect']].median() if x[df_pesticides.loc[x.index, 'Is_Detect']].size > 0 else np.nan) ).reset_index() # Function to normalize units for comparison to PCV in ug/L def convert_to_ugL(row): units = str(row['Units']).lower() val_max = row['max_detect'] val_median = row['median_detect'] if pd.isnull(val_max) or pd.isnull(val_median): return pd.Series([np.nan, np.nan]) if any(u in units for u in ['µg/l', 'μg/l', 'ug/l', 'î¼g/l', 'ï¿¼g/l', 'î»g/l', 'µg/l']): # Already micrograms per litre, no conversion return pd.Series([val_max, val_median]) elif 'mg/l' in units: # Convert mg/L to ug/L return pd.Series([val_max * 1000, val_median * 1000]) else: # Unknown units conversion, use original values (warning: may be invalid) return pd.Series([val_max, val_median]) # Apply conversion agg[['max_detect_ugL', 'median_detect_ugL']] = agg.apply(convert_to_ugL, axis=1) # Add PCV columns according to rules def assign_pcvs(determin): pcv_individual = np.nan pcv_total = np.nan if determin == "Pesticides Aldrin": pcv_individual = 0.03 elif determin == "Pesticides Dieldrin (Total)": pcv_individual = 0.03 elif determin == "Pesticides Heptachlor (Total)": pcv_individual = 0.03 elif determin == "Pesticides Heptachlor Epoxide - Total (Trans, CIS) (Heptachlor Epoxide)": pcv_individual = 0.03 elif determin == "Pesticides (Total by Calculation)": pcv_total = 0.5 else: # For all other pesticides except total pesticides (and not one of the above) if determin.startswith("Pesticides "): pcv_individual = 0.1 return pd.Series([pcv_individual, pcv_total]) agg[['PCV_ugL_individual', 'PCV_ugL_total']] = agg['Determinand'].apply(assign_pcvs) # Save to CSV output_file = "pesticides_summary_stats.csv" agg.to_csv(output_file, index=False) print(f"pesticides_summary_stats.csv") print(f"pesticides_summary_stats.py") ``` ####Chart: Time series of individual pesticide detections at consumers’ taps (excluding total pesticides) This chart shows only quantified detections of individual pesticides in 2022, with the total pesticides (by calculation) series removed. Metaldehyde dominates, with 17 detections ranging from about 0.008 to 0.037 µg/L, spread throughout the year and across multiple LSOAs. Propyzamide is detected on four occasions (approximately 0.005–0.024 µg/L), typically coinciding with metaldehyde detections in the same LSOAs, indicating common catchment or seasonal drivers. Atrazine appears twice at 0.004 µg/L, and 2,4‑D appears once at 0.016 µg/L, all as isolated low-level events. All results are well below the 0.1 µg/L UK PCV for individual pesticides, and none show an upward trend over the year, suggesting persistent but low-intensity diffuse inputs rather than acute contamination episodes. Chart image: . Code used to create component: ```python # filename: pesticide_detections_timeseries.py import pandas as pd import plotly.express as px # Load data file_name = "Anglian_Water_Domestic_Water_Quality.csv" df = pd.read_csv(file_name, encoding="utf-8") # Convert Sample_Date to datetime df['Sample_Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Sample_Date'], format="%d/%m/%Y %H:%M", errors='coerce') # Filter pesticide determinands: only those starting with "Pesticides " to exclude total by calculation mask_pesticides = df['Determinand'].str.startswith("Pesticides ", na=False) # Remove the "Pesticides (Total by Calculation)" series explicitly df_pesticides = df.loc[mask_pesticides & (df['Determinand'] != "Pesticides (Total by Calculation)")].copy() # Define detections: Operator not '<' df_pesticides['Is_Detect'] = ~df_pesticides['Operator'].eq('<') # Find determinands with at least one detection (n_detects > 0) detection_counts = df_pesticides.groupby('Determinand')['Is_Detect'].sum() valid_determinands = detection_counts[detection_counts > 0].index # Filter to only include detected pesticide determinands df_detections = df_pesticides[df_pesticides['Determinand'].isin(valid_determinands) & df_pesticides['Is_Detect']] # Keep only needed columns for plotting df_plot = df_detections[['Sample_Date', 'Determinand', 'Result', 'Units', 'DWI_Code', 'LSOA']].copy() # Save CSV file containing data plotted csv_output = "pesticide_detections_timeseries.csv" df_plot.to_csv(csv_output, index=False) # Create Plotly scatter plot with markers only, color by Determinand fig = px.scatter( df_plot, x='Sample_Date', y='Result', color='Determinand', labels={'Sample_Date': 'Sample Date', 'Result': 'Result'}, hover_data={'Units': True, 'DWI_Code': True, 'LSOA': True}, title='', # no title as requested template='plotly_white', ) # Update layout for gridlines, font, and black axis lines/tick marks fig.update_layout( font=dict(size=16), xaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor='lightgrey', linecolor='black', mirror=True, ticks='outside'), yaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor='lightgrey', linecolor='black', mirror=True, ticks='outside'), legend_title_text='Determinand', ) # Resize plotly image dimensions when saved png img_output = "pesticide_detections_timeseries.png" html_output = "pesticide_detections_timeseries.html" # Save figure html with plotlyjs='cdn' and responsive fig.write_html(html_output, include_plotlyjs='cdn', full_html=True) # Save figure to png with size 1000x600 fig.write_image(img_output, width=1000, height=600) print(csv_output) print(html_output) print(img_output) print("pesticide_detections_timeseries.py") ```
name: function_agent

content:
<process_agent>:
The chart itself must be changed.

Here are the explicit modification instructions for the coding agent:

1. **Change the default zoom / map extent**  
   - Remove the fixed `zoom=9` argument in the `px.scatter_mapbox` call, or replace it with a zoom derived from the full spatial extent of `df_map`.  
   - Use one of the following approaches so that, on load, the map shows **all LSOAs with pesticide detections**:
     - Compute min/max of `lat` and `lon` from `df_map` and set the map center and zoom accordingly using `fig.update_layout(mapbox_center=..., mapbox_zoom=...)` so that all points are visible, or  
     - Use Plotly’s `fitbounds="locations"` option in `px.scatter_mapbox` to auto-fit the map to all marker locations.

2. Ensure that after this change, the default view in the HTML/PNG clearly displays the full spatial spread of all LSOA points with detections, not just a single local cluster.
name: process_agent

content: : Here is the chart you requested (chart filename is lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.png): . The html version of this chart is available at lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.html. Additional information (stored in not applicable): No additional information provided. This chart was created from this code (stored in lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.py): # filename: lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.py import pandas as pd import numpy as np import plotly.express as px # Main water quality data filename water_quality_file = "Anglian_Water_Domestic_Water_Quality.csv" # Load main data df = pd.read_csv(water_quality_file, encoding="utf-8") # Convert Sample_Date to datetime df['Sample_Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Sample_Date'], format="%d/%m/%Y %H:%M", errors='coerce') # Filter pesticide determinands: mask_pesticides = df['Determinand'].str.startswith("Pesticides ", na=False) | (df['Determinand'] == "Pesticides (Total by Calculation)") df_pesticides = df.loc[mask_pesticides].copy() # Define detections: Operator not '<' df_pesticides['Is_Detect'] = ~df_pesticides['Operator'].eq('<') # Calculate detection counts per determinand detection_counts = df_pesticides.groupby('Determinand')['Is_Detect'].sum() valid_determinands = detection_counts[detection_counts > 0].index # Filter only to valid detected pesticide determinands and only detects df_valid_detections = df_pesticides[(df_pesticides['Determinand'].isin(valid_determinands)) & (df_pesticides['Is_Detect'])] # Group by LSOA to count detections lsoa_counts = df_valid_detections.groupby('LSOA').size().reset_index(name='detection_count') # Load ONS LSOA centroid CSV ons_lsoa_url = "https://open-geography-portalx-ons.hub.arcgis.com/api/download/v1/items/b8263c2364e9452483a0e5783c6fdb53/csv?layers=0" df_lsoa = pd.read_csv(ons_lsoa_url, encoding='utf-8') # Identify latitude and longitude column names (try common variants) lat_cols = [col for col in df_lsoa.columns if col.lower() in {'lat', 'latitude', 'y'}] lon_cols = [col for col in df_lsoa.columns if col.lower() in {'lon', 'long', 'longitude', 'x'}] if not lat_cols or not lon_cols: raise ValueError("Latitude and/or Longitude columns not found in LSOA centroids data.") lat_col = lat_cols[0] lon_col = lon_cols[0] # Select and rename to common names df_lsoa_selected = df_lsoa[['LSOA21CD', 'LSOA21NM', lat_col, lon_col]].rename( columns={lat_col: 'lat', lon_col: 'lon'} ) # Inner join detection counts with LSOA centroids on code df_map = pd.merge(lsoa_counts, df_lsoa_selected, how='inner', left_on='LSOA', right_on='LSOA21CD') # Sort by detection_count ascending so higher counts are plotted last (on top) df_map = df_map.sort_values('detection_count') # Marker size scale: detection_count mapped from [min, max] -> [5, 25] min_size = 5 max_size = 25 min_count = df_map['detection_count'].min() max_count = df_map['detection_count'].max() # Avoid division by zero if all counts are same if max_count == min_count: df_map['marker_size'] = (min_size + max_size) / 2 else: df_map['marker_size'] = min_size + (df_map['detection_count'] - min_count) / (max_count - min_count) * (max_size - min_size) # Compute map center as the midpoint of lat and lon center_lat = df_map['lat'].mean() center_lon = df_map['lon'].mean() # Compute latitude and longitude ranges to estimate zoom level lat_range = df_map['lat'].max() - df_map['lat'].min() lon_range = df_map['lon'].max() - df_map['lon'].min() # Approximate zoom level based on max range - empirical formula for Mapbox zoom levels # Larger range means smaller zoom max_range = max(lat_range, lon_range) if max_range == 0: zoom_level = 10 # arbitrary default for single point else: # zoom_level decreases as max_range increases # Reference: zoom level approximately = 8 - log2(range), adjusted for suitable appearance import math zoom_level = 8 - np.log2(max_range) if zoom_level < 3: zoom_level = 3 # minimum zoom to avoid zooming out too far elif zoom_level > 12: zoom_level = 12 # maximum zoom to avoid zooming in too far # Use Plotly Mapbox scatter plot for heat map style fig = px.scatter_mapbox( df_map, lat='lat', lon='lon', size='marker_size', size_max=max_size, color='detection_count', color_continuous_scale='YlOrRd', hover_name='LSOA21NM', hover_data={'LSOA21CD': True, 'detection_count': True, 'lat': False, 'lon': False, 'marker_size': False}, zoom=zoom_level, height=600, width=1000, labels={'detection_count': 'Detection Count'}, mapbox_style='open-street-map' ) # Update layout to remove title and add black lines (axes lines are not shown on mapbox plots) fig.update_layout( font=dict(size=16), margin={"r":0,"t":0,"l":0,"b":0}, coloraxis_colorbar=dict(title="Detections"), mapbox_center=dict(lat=center_lat, lon=center_lon) ) # Save outputs csv_file = "lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap_data.csv" html_file = "lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.html" png_file = "lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.png" df_map[['LSOA21CD', 'LSOA21NM', 'detection_count', 'lat', 'lon']].to_csv(csv_file, index=False) fig.write_html(html_file, include_plotlyjs='cdn', full_html=True) fig.write_image(png_file, width=1000, height=600) print(csv_file) print(html_file) print(png_file) print("lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.py") Let me know if you would like any changes or improvements. If the chart is ok, make a tool call to add_chart_and_comment.'
name: code_som_agent

function.arguments: {"analysis_index":24,"item_index":2,"title":"LSOA heat map of pesticide detection counts","analysis_comments":"Pesticide detections are spatially sparse, with only 22 LSOAs showing any quantified hits in 2022. Most have 1–2 detections, indicating isolated low‑level events rather than widespread contamination. The highest counts cluster around Colchester: LSOA Colchester 019A has 7 detections, while neighbouring Colchester 019B, 020A, 020B and 020E each show 2–4 detections, pointing to a persistent source in that supply zone. South Cambridgeshire 003E and Central Bedfordshire 018D both register 6 detections, forming additional hotspots. A Milton Keynes group (032A, 032C–032F) records 1–4 detections each, suggesting diffuse but recurring pesticide presence. Northern outliers at Doncaster (up to 6 detections in 026D) and Bassetlaw (up to 4 in 015D) are geographically separated from the main eastern clusters. Overall, patterns are consistent with catchment‑specific pressures rather than system‑wide pesticide problems.","chart_filename":"lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.png","chart_html_filename":"lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.html","code_filename":"lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap.py","data_filename":"lsoa_pesticide_detection_heatmap_data.csv"}
function.name: revise_chart
